section.title: Salvation for the Foreigner
section.sectionRationale: Having thrown open a free invitation to the thirsty in chapter 55, Isaiah now names exactly who may walk through that door, even the eunuch, barred from the assembly under the law, and the foreigner, once outside the covenant altogether. This is the seam where Isaiah's universal vision of a house of prayer for all nations comes into sharpest focus.
section.thesis: God welcomes the eunuch and the foreigner who hold fast to His covenant and keep His Sabbath, giving them an everlasting name and place in His house, because His house was never meant to be for one people only but a house of prayer for all nations.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to justice and Sabbath-keeping as salvation draws near; the eunuch and foreigner told not to count themselves excluded; and God's house declared a house of prayer for all nations.

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “Maintain justice and do what is right, for My salvation is coming soon, and My righteousness will be revealed.
Blessed is the man who does this, and the son of man who holds it fast, who keeps the Sabbath without profaning it and keeps his hand from doing any evil.”

units.label: Do not say I am but a dry tree

verses.text: Let no {{nekhar|foreigner}} who has joined himself to the LORD say, “The LORD will utterly exclude me from His people.” And let the {{saris|eunuch}} not say, “I am but a dry tree.”
For this is what the LORD says: “To the eunuchs who keep My Sabbaths, who choose what pleases Me and hold fast to My covenant,
I will give them, in My house and within My walls, a memorial and a name better than that of sons and daughters. I will give them an everlasting name that will not be cut off.

And the foreigners who join themselves to the LORD to minister to Him, to love the name of the LORD, and to be His servants, all who keep the Sabbath without profaning it and who hold fast to My covenant,

units.label: A house of prayer for all nations

verses.text: I will bring them to My holy mountain and make them joyful in My house of prayer. Their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on My altar, for My house will be called a house of prayer for all the nations.”
Thus declares the Lord GOD, who gathers the dispersed of Israel: “I will gather to them still others besides those already gathered.”

nekhar.gloss: Foreigner, one from outside Israel's ethnic and covenant boundary. Under the law such a person could be marginal to Israel's worship; here God explicitly welcomes the foreigner who joins himself to the LORD, loves His name, and keeps His covenant, foreshadowing the full inclusion of the nations in Christ.

saris.gloss: Eunuch, a man excluded from full assembly and unable to have descendants, Deuteronomy 23:1. God promises such a man, in His house, a memorial and an everlasting name better than sons and daughters, directly answering his deepest fear of being cut off and forgotten.

basic.context: After inviting all who thirst in chapter 55, Isaiah names two groups the law seemed to shut out, the eunuch, who could not enter the assembly, and the foreigner, outside Israel's covenant people. God tells both plainly not to count themselves excluded; He will give them a place, a name, and joy in His house.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage does not overturn the law lightly, it shows the law's own deeper purpose, a people set apart to display God's holiness so that outsiders could be drawn in, not shut out. Faithful Sabbath-keeping and covenant-holding, not ethnicity or physical wholeness, become the mark of belonging.
basic.originalLanguages: The nekhar, foreigner, and the saris, eunuch, both marginal or barred figures under Israel's law, are here promised full welcome if they hold fast to God's covenant. The eunuch, who could leave no descendants, is given something better, an everlasting name in God's own house.
basic.typology: This anticipates the full inclusion of Gentiles, and of formerly excluded outsiders, into the people of God through Christ, and is fulfilled literally when Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch, himself reading Isaiah, in Acts 8.
basic.christ: Jesus quotes verse 7 directly, my house will be called a house of prayer, when He cleanses the temple, adding the phrase for all nations that the religious leaders had let the court of the Gentiles become a marketplace instead, Mark 11:17. The Ethiopian eunuch of Acts 8, reading Isaiah, becomes a living fulfillment of this very promise, welcomed and baptized though he was exactly the kind of person verse 3 addresses.
basic.god: God's holiness does not exclude the marginal, it welcomes them on the basis of covenant faithfulness rather than physical status or ethnic origin. He is a God who gathers still others besides those already gathered, His house never intended to close its doors.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 11:17 has Jesus quote this passage directly at the temple's cleansing. Acts 8:26-39 shows an actual eunuch, reading Isaiah, welcomed into God's people. Ephesians 2:11-13 describes Gentiles, once far off, brought near by the blood of Christ.
basic.oneStory: From the very people the law seemed to shut out, Isaiah promises a name, a place, and joy in God's own house. That promise becomes flesh in the New Testament when an actual eunuch is baptized and Gentiles are grafted in, proving that God's house was always meant to be a house of prayer for all nations.

context.literary: A tightly built oracle moving from a call to justice and Sabbath-keeping, to direct address reassuring the eunuch and foreigner, to a climactic declaration that God's house is a house of prayer for all nations, closing the section that began with chapter 55's open invitation.
context.historical: Addressed to a post-exilic community rebuilding its identity around the law and temple; Deuteronomy 23:1-3 had restricted eunuchs and certain foreigners from full assembly, so this promise is a striking, deliberate widening of who may belong, not a casual afterthought.
context.canonical: Quoted directly by Jesus in His temple cleansing, Mark 11:17, and enacted historically in Acts 8 when Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch, making this one of the clearest Old Testament anticipations of the gospel's reach to all nations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure eunuchs and foreigners who sincerely hold fast to God's covenant that they have full standing and honor in His house, correcting any assumption that physical wholeness or Israelite ancestry determines who belongs to God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage both promises, God will welcome and give an everlasting name, and prescribes, keep the Sabbath, hold fast to the covenant, love the name of the LORD, showing that the welcome is real and gracious even as it calls for genuine covenant faithfulness in response.

debates.question: Does this passage abolish the restrictions of Deuteronomy 23:1 outright?

views.label: A direct reversal
views.case: Deuteronomy 23:1 barred eunuchs from the assembly; Isaiah 56 seems to explicitly overturn that exclusion for eunuchs who keep covenant.

views.label: A revealed deeper intent, not a contradiction
views.case: The Deuteronomy law functioned within a specific covenant economy pointing toward wholeness and holiness; Isaiah reveals that the true qualification was always covenant faithfulness, which the eunuch can now fully display, especially as the age of fulfillment in the Messiah dawns.

debates.bearing: Either way, the trajectory is the same: God's house was always moving toward wider inclusion for those who hold fast to Him, a trajectory that reaches its fullness in Christ, where neither ethnicity nor physical status is a barrier, Galatians 3:28.

study.originalLanguages: Nekhar, the foreigner, and saris, the eunuch, name two groups on the very edge of covenant belonging under the law. Both are promised full welcome, provided they hold fast to God's covenant and keep His Sabbath, showing that the qualification for belonging was always faithfulness rather than birth or bodily wholeness.

typology.type: The eunuch given an everlasting name in God's house, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: The Ethiopian eunuch of Acts 8, reading Isaiah, baptized and welcomed into the church.

typology.type: A house of prayer for all nations, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' cleansing of the temple, Mark 11:17, and the church as a temple built of Jew and Gentile together, Ephesians 2:19-22.

study.christ: Jesus quotes this very passage when He drives out the money changers, declaring my house will be called a house of prayer for all nations, and rebuking the leaders for turning the court set aside for Gentile worshippers into a marketplace, Mark 11:17. The literal fulfillment follows in Acts 8, where an Ethiopian eunuch, reading the scroll of Isaiah, is welcomed, believes, and is baptized, embodying exactly the promise of verses 3 to 5. In Christ, the boundary lines of ethnicity and physical status that once excluded are torn down, Galatians 3:28, Ephesians 2:14.
study.god: God defines belonging by covenant faithfulness, not by ancestry or bodily condition, and He delights to gather still others besides those already gathered, verse 8, a house of prayer whose doors keep widening rather than closing.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes Isaiah 56:7 directly, my house will be called a house of prayer for all nations.
An Ethiopian eunuch, reading Isaiah, is welcomed and baptized, a living fulfillment of this passage.
The earlier restriction on eunuchs entering the assembly, against which this promise stands in striking relief.
In Christ there is neither slave nor free, echoing the removal of exclusionary boundaries.

study.oneStory: God names the very people the law seemed to shut out, the eunuch and the foreigner, and promises them a name and a place better than sons and daughters. Jesus quotes this promise at the temple, and the book of Acts shows it coming true in a real eunuch's baptism, proof that God's house was always meant to be a house of prayer for all nations.

quiz.q: What does God promise to the eunuch who keeps His Sabbaths and holds fast to His covenant?

options.text: A memorial and an everlasting name in God's house, better than sons and daughters.
options.note: Verse 5 directly answers the eunuch's fear of being cut off without descendants.

options.text: Physical healing so he is no longer a eunuch.
options.note: This addresses his real condition, but the promise given is a name and place in God's house, not a physical change.

options.text: Exclusion from the assembly, matching Deuteronomy's restriction.
options.note: The passage reverses this expectation, promising full welcome.

options.text: A temporary place outside the temple courts.
options.note: The promise is to be within God's house and walls, not kept at a distance.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use Isaiah 56:7 in the Gospels?

options.text: He quotes it while cleansing the temple, rebuking leaders for turning a house of prayer for all nations into a marketplace.
He quotes it to explain a parable about seeds.
options.note: It is quoted specifically at the temple cleansing.

options.text: He uses it to argue foreigners should never enter the temple.
options.note: This is the opposite of Jesus' point; He emphasizes the phrase for all nations to rebuke exclusion, not enforce it.

options.text: It is never quoted in the Gospels.

quiz.q: What qualification does the passage give for the foreigner's welcome into God's house?

options.text: Joining himself to the LORD, loving His name, and keeping His covenant and Sabbath, not ethnicity.
options.note: Verses 3-6 describe covenant faithfulness as the qualifying mark, not birth.

options.text: First becoming ethnically Israelite through marriage.
options.note: The text names covenant faithfulness, not ethnic assimilation, as the mark of belonging.

options.text: Bringing enough wealth to the temple treasury.
options.note: The passage speaks of loving the LORD's name and covenant faithfulness as the qualification, not wealth brought to the treasury.

options.text: No qualification is given; all foreigners are welcomed unconditionally regardless of belief.
options.note: The passage does specify conditions, joining himself to the LORD and holding fast to His covenant.

quiz.q: What event in Acts embodies the promise of Isaiah 56:3-5?

options.text: Philip's encounter with the Ethiopian eunuch, who is reading Isaiah and is baptized.
options.note: Acts 8:26-39 is a striking, literal fulfillment of the eunuch's welcome promised here.

options.text: Peter's vision of the sheet of animals in Acts 10.
options.note: That passage concerns clean and unclean food and Gentile inclusion generally, but Acts 8's eunuch is the direct fulfillment of this specific promise.

options.text: The stoning of Stephen in Acts 7.
options.note: That event is unrelated to the eunuch and foreigner promise of Isaiah 56.

options.text: Paul's shipwreck on Malta in Acts 27.
options.note: Unrelated to this passage's specific promise.

questions: The eunuch feared being called a dry tree, cut off with no future. What fear of being excluded or forgotten do you carry that God is inviting you to bring to Him?
God's welcome here is tied to genuinely holding fast to His covenant, not merely proximity to His people. Is your own faith a matter of genuine covenant devotion, or mostly cultural belonging?
Jesus quoted this passage to rebuke a temple that had crowded out the very people it was meant to welcome. Where might you be, even unintentionally, making it harder for an outsider to approach God?
God says He will gather still others besides those already gathered, verse 8. Who is someone outside your usual circle that God might be calling you to welcome or reach out to?
The eunuch was promised something better than sons and daughters, a name in God's own house. Where are you tempted to measure your worth by what you lack rather than by your place in God's family?

translationNotes.note: Blessed is the man, and the son of man, uses inclusive Hebrew idiom for humanity generally, not a title for a specific figure, distinct from the later apocalyptic use of son of man.
Dry tree renders a Hebrew idiom for one who leaves no descendants, capturing the eunuch's particular grief under the old covenant's expectations of family lineage.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 56:1-8; no em dashes present in this passage.
